medications.indication_rationale: Analgesia is the mainstay of AOM. Regular weight-based paracetamol ± ibuprofen controls pain and fever — BUT this is a child, so the dose must be weight-based and prescribed at in-person/GP review; a remote dose is NOT synthesised here (protocol §8).

A delayed-antibiotic (fill-only-if-not-improving-in-48h) amoxicillin script is appropriate for mild AOM in a well child; the dose is weight-based and prescribed in person. Immediate antibiotics reserved for severe/bilateral-under-2/otorrhoea/high-risk.

medications.drug_name: immediate antibiotics for mild AOM in a well child
medications.indication_rationale: Up-front antibiotics are not recommended for mild AOM in an otherwise-well child >6 months — analgesia-first / delayed antibiotics is preferred to reduce resistance.
No evidence of benefit in AOM and not recommended in young children.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Give analgesia regularly (at the correct weight-based dose from your clinician/pharmacist) and write down each dose and time to avoid double-dosing; measure temperature with a thermometer.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Regular analgesia controls pain; documenting doses prevents errors by tired parents.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the child slightly upright for sleep and offer frequent fluids; do not put anything in the ear.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces pressure pain and maintains hydration.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a mild ear infection after his cold. The pain comes from pressure behind the eardrum; regular pain relief is the main treatment. Most mild ear infections settle in 2–3 days without antibiotics.

We'll give a 'wait-and-see' antibiotic script to fill ONLY if he isn't improving after 48 hours or gets worse — that avoids unnecessary antibiotics. His pain-relief dose needs to be worked out by weight with your GP/pharmacist.

Go to ED / call 000 if a red tender swelling appears on the bone behind the ear or the ear looks pushed forward, if he becomes drowsy/hard to wake, develops a stiff neck or a rash, stops drinking / has fewer wet nappies, or vomits repeatedly.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Otoscopy, pain/fever course, whether the delayed antibiotic was needed; hearing.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Mastoiditis/meningitis features, not drinking, or repeated vomiting — ED.

must_include_items: Analgesia-first management with a delayed/watchful-waiting antibiotic plan (not immediate antibiotics for mild AOM)
Defer paediatric analgesic/antibiotic dosing to weight-based in-person prescribing
acceptable_alternatives: Immediate antibiotics IF severe/bilateral-under-2/otorrhoea/high-risk emerges; amoxicillin as the agent
errors_of_commission: Immediate antibiotics for mild AOM in a well child
Synthesising a specific paediatric dose remotely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires an analgesia-first/delayed-antibiotic approach, a mastoiditis/meningitis safety-net, and NOT producing a remote paediatric dose.
